citations.narrative:
Online social media photos show members of the public interacting with a young serval at the licensee’s facility. A post
from a public member (01/18/2026) shows several pictures of at least two public members holding the leash of and
petting a young serval. A video (01/21/2026) from the facility’s social media page advertises an opportunity to be a
“Keeper for a day” and includes a clip of a young child interacting with a young serval. An additional post (01/24/2026) by
the facility invites the public to come visit their animals; one of the pictures attached includes one of a very young serval.
Another post (02/22/2026) makes reference to the recovery process for a serval that underwent surgery three weeks prior
and that the facility identifies as theirs. The licensee explained that the serval is too young to reside in an enclosure by
herself so she resides at the home of one of the senior staff members. The licensee’s current 3-year license does not
have authorization for wild and exotic felines. Not acquiring the correct license designations prior to obtaining restricted
animal species can result in animals being held by unauthorized or untrained personnel for the species. Per the
regulations, licenses authorize the use of animals subject to subparts A through F in part 3 of this subchapter, except that,
for animals subject to subparts D and F, licenses must specifically authorize the use of each of the following groups of
animals: Group 5 (baboons and nonbrachiating species larger than 33 lbs.) and Group 6 (great apes over 55 lbs. and
brachiating species) nonhuman primates; exotic and wild felids (including but not limited to lions, tigers, leopards,
cheetahs, jaguars, cougars, lynx, servals, bobcats, and caracals, and any hybrid cross thereof); hyenas and/or exotic and
wild canids (including but not limited to wolves, coyotes, foxes, and jackals); bears; and mega-herbivores (including but
not limited to elephants, rhinoceroses, hippopotamuses, and giraffes). A licensee must obtain a new license before using
any animal beyond those types or numbers of animals authorized under the existing license.
Correct by April 13th, 2026.

The facility allowed members of the public to interact with their black and white ruffed lemurs in an unsafe manner. The
facility offers various types of public interactions with multiple species of lemurs. The lemurs are trained using verbal recall
to return to their primary enclosures when staff deem situations unsafe. Online photos taken by public members dated
01/05/2026 show a white and black ruffed lemur perched on a public member’s head and shoulder. A public member can
been seen offering a piece of food over the head of another woman and encouraging the lemur to perch onto the sitting
woman's head. Another set of photos posted on 12/23/2025 by a different public member during a "Keeper for a Day"
experience show a black and white ruffed lemur held close to the woman's face and being kissed on the back by the
public member. Allowing nonhuman primates to perch on or near public member’s head and shoulders increases the
likelihood of injury to both the animals and the public. Additionally, this type of close contact increases the spread of
zoonotic disease. During public exhibition, any animal must be handled so there is minimal risk of harm to the animal and
to the public.

During inspection, APHIS Officials observed non-human primates having access to inappropriate materials. The open top
enrichment enclosure on the front of the property had an exposed extension cord running from the power source to the
muntjac’s den to provide warmth during low temperatures. Access to foreign items such as electrical cords can result in
illness or injury to the animals. Housing facilities must be kept in good repair and protect the animals from injury.

APHIS Officials identified rust in the transfer tunnels from the primary enclosures for several species of lemurs to the open
enrichment enclosure. The most severe rusted area had multiple sharp points created where the metal had disintegrated.
APHIS Officials also identified a rusted metal cage panel affixed to the outside of an enclosure housing lemurs. The
facility representative stated they explained the panels were formerly used it to attach the fans during the summer. No fan
was attached at the time of inspection, and so the lemurs had direct access to the rusted panel. The presence of
excessive rust in areas where nonhuman primates have direct access cannot be cleaned and sanitized as required
appropriately. Additionally, excessively rusted metal loses its integrity and may create sharp points that pose harm to the
animals.
Furniture-type fixtures or objects must be sturdily constructed and must be strong enough to provide the safe activity and
welfare of nonhuman primates. Any surfaces that come in contact with nonhuman primates must be free of excessive rust
that prevents the required cleaning and sanitization, or that affects the structural strength of the surface.
Correct by March 30, 2026.

In the sheltered portion of the squirrel monkeys’ area, APHIS Officials identified a bag of Mizzou Primate dry food placed
directly on the floor. Upon further inspection, the bag still contained food in it. Additionally, cleaning chemicals had been
placed directly next to opened bags of cashews, peanuts and canned chicken on a shelf above the sink area. Storage of
food stuff directly near cleaning chemicals poses a cross-contamination hazard that may be harmful when ingested.
Additionally, placing open bags of food directly on the floor may expose the food to contamination by pests and make it
difficult to allow for effective cleaning practices.
Supplies of food and bedding must be stored in a manner that protects the supplies from spoilage, contamination, and
vermin infestation. The supplies must be stored off the floor and away from the walls, to allow cleaning underneath and
around the supplies. Only the food and bedding currently being used may be kept in animal areas, and when not in actual
use, open food and bedding supplies must be kept in leakproof containers with tightly fitting lids to prevent spoilage and
contamination. Substances that are toxic to the nonhuman primates but that are required for normal husbandry practices
must not be stored in food storage and preparation areas but may be stored in cabinets in the animal areas. Any opened
food container must be kept in leakproof containers with tightly fitting lids.
Corrected at the time of inspection.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
Additional Inspectors:
Patricia Laracuente, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:

2.131(c)(1)  Repeat
   
Handling of animals. 
The facility allowed members of the public to interact with their black and white ruffed lemurs in an unsafe manner. The

3.75(a)  Repeat

3.75(c)(1)(i) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
APHIS Officials identified rust in the transfer tunnels from the primary enclosures for several species of lemurs to the open

APHIS Officials also identified a rusted metal cage panel affixed to the outside of an enclosure housing lemurs. The

Correct by March 30, 2026. 
3.75(e) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
In the sheltered portion of the squirrel monkeys’ area, APHIS Officials identified a bag of Mizzou Primate dry food placed

Supplies of food and bedding must be stored in a manner that protects the supplies from spoilage, contamination, and

This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.

species.common: CONGO AFRICAN GREY PARROT / GRAY PARROT
